title: The Face of Battle: A Study of Agincourt, Waterloo, and the Somme
excerpt: A groundbreaking work of military history that shifts the focus from generals and strategy to the lived experience of ordinary soldiers in combat, examining three iconic battles across six centuries.
keyIdeas: Traditional military history focuses on generals and maps but ignores what actually happens to soldiers in battle
Battle is a profoundly disorienting, terrifying, and chaotic experience that bears little resemblance to drill-ground order
Understanding battle requires attention to psychology, sociology, and the physical realities of combat
The experience of battle has changed fundamentally across history as weapons and tactics evolved
keyTakeaways: Agincourt (1415): Medieval battle was a brutal, close-quarters physical confrontation shaped by armor, archery, and chivalric values
Waterloo (1815): Napoleonic battle was a mass encounter of close-order infantry, artillery, and cavalry, dominated by noise, smoke, and courage
The Somme (1916): Industrial battle was a depersonalized, mechanized slaughter where individual skill mattered less than industrial capacity
As battles have become more destructive, soldiers have become less willing to expose themselves to fire, creating new tactical challenges
whoShouldRead: Military history enthusiasts wanting to understand what battle actually felt like for ordinary soldiers
Historians seeking a model for bottom-up social history applied to military subjects
General readers interested in the human experience of war beyond tactics and strategy
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for a comprehensive strategic or operational history of any of the three battles
Those wanting a continuous narrative — the book is three separate case studies with analytical framing
Anyone expecting a celebratory or romantic account of battle — Keegan emphasizes horror, confusion, and suffering
